hhdynamics 1.3.1

Statistical Methodology

Source: vignettes/hhdynamics-methodology.Rmd
hhdynamics-methodology.Rmd

Overview

This vignette describes the statistical model, likelihood, prior distributions, and MCMC algorithm implemented in hhdynamics. For a practical workflow guide, see vignette("hhdynamics-intro").

The model is a discrete-time Bayesian household transmission model that separates community and within-household infection sources. It was originally described in Tsang et al. (2014) and has been extended here to support covariate effects, missing data imputation, and optional serial interval estimation.

Model

Study design

The data come from a case-ascertained household study. Each household is enrolled through an index case (the first identified case), and household contacts are followed prospectively for a defined period. The observed data for each contact include whether they became infected and, if so, the day of symptom onset. Covariates (age, sex, etc.) may be recorded for each individual.

Infection hazard

For household contact ii in household hh on day tt, the hazard of infection is:

λi(t)=[βc+∑j∈ℐhj≠iβh⋅w(t−tj)⋅exp(𝐱j⊤𝛂inf+εj)(nh−1)γ]⋅exp(𝐳i⊤𝛂sus) \lambda_i(t) = \left[\beta_c + \sum_{\substack{j \in \mathcal{I}_h \\ j \neq i}} \frac{\beta_h \cdot w(t - t_j) \cdot \exp(\mathbf{x}_j^\top \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{\text{inf}} + \varepsilon_j)}{(n_h - 1)^\gamma}\right] \cdot \exp(\mathbf{z}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{\text{sus}})

where:

  • βc>0\beta_c > 0 is the daily rate of infection from the community, assumed constant over the follow-up period.
  • βh>0\beta_h > 0 is the baseline rate of person-to-person transmission within households.
  • w(⋅)w(\cdot) is the serial interval distribution: a discrete probability mass function where w(d)w(d) gives the probability of transmission dd days after the infector’s symptom onset. By default, a 14-day influenza serial interval from Tsang et al. (2014) is used.
  • tjt_j is the symptom onset day of infected household member jj.
  • ℐh\mathcal{I}_h is the set of all infected members in household hh (including the index case and any tertiary cases).
  • 𝐱j\mathbf{x}_j is the vector of infectivity covariates for person jj, and 𝛂inf\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{\text{inf}} are the corresponding log-scale effects.
  • εj∼Normal(0,σε2)\varepsilon_j \sim \text{Normal}(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2) is an optional individual-level random effect on infectivity (σε\sigma_\varepsilon is estimated; disabled by default).
  • nhn_h is the household size and γ≥0\gamma \geq 0 is a scaling parameter (currently fixed at 0, so there is no frequency-dependent adjustment).
  • 𝐳i\mathbf{z}_i is the vector of susceptibility covariates for person ii, and 𝛂sus\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{\text{sus}} are the corresponding log-scale effects.

The daily probability that contact ii becomes infected on day tt, given they are still susceptible, is:

P(infected on day t∣susceptible at t)=1−exp(−λi(t)) P(\text{infected on day } t \mid \text{susceptible at } t) = 1 - \exp(-\lambda_i(t))

Covariates

Covariate effects enter the hazard multiplicatively on the log scale:

  • Infectivity covariates (𝐱j\mathbf{x}_j): modify the transmission rate of the infector. A positive αinf,k\alpha_{\text{inf},k} means that level increases the hazard of infecting others. The exponentiated coefficient exp(αinf,k)\exp(\alpha_{\text{inf},k}) is the relative infectiousness compared to the reference level.
  • Susceptibility covariates (𝐳i\mathbf{z}_i): modify the susceptibility of the contact. A positive αsus,k\alpha_{\text{sus},k} means that level is more susceptible. The exponentiated coefficient exp(αsus,k)\exp(\alpha_{\text{sus},k}) is the relative susceptibility compared to the reference level.

Factor covariates are encoded as dummy variables using treatment contrasts (the first level is the reference).

Likelihood

Individual likelihood

For contact ii in household hh, let TiT_i be the infection day (if infected) or the end of follow-up plus one (if not infected), and let shs_h be the start of the observation period (the index case’s onset day). Let δi=1\delta_i = 1 if infected, 00 otherwise.

The log-likelihood contribution of contact ii is:

ℓi=−∑t=shTi−2λi(t)+δi⋅log(1−exp(−λi(Ti−1))) \ell_i = -\sum_{t=s_h}^{T_i - 2} \lambda_i(t) + \delta_i \cdot \log\left(1 - \exp(-\lambda_i(T_i - 1))\right)

The first term captures the survival probability (not being infected on days before TiT_i), and the second term captures the probability of infection on day TiT_i (only included if the contact was actually infected).

Full likelihood

The full data log-likelihood is the sum over all non-index contacts in all households:

ℓ(𝛉)=∑h=1H∑i=1nh−1ℓi \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{h=1}^{H} \sum_{i=1}^{n_h - 1} \ell_i

where 𝛉=(βc,βh,γ,𝛂inf,𝛂sus,σε)\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\beta_c, \beta_h, \gamma, \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{\text{inf}}, \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{\text{sus}}, \sigma_\varepsilon) is the full parameter vector.

When random effects are included, the likelihood also includes the random effects density:

ℓRE=∑h=1H∑j∈ℐhlogϕ(εj;0,σε2) \ell_{\text{RE}} = \sum_{h=1}^{H} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{I}_h} \log \phi(\varepsilon_j; 0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)

where ϕ(⋅;0,σ2)\phi(\cdot; 0, \sigma^2) is the normal density.

Prior distributions

Parameter Prior Description
σε\sigma_\varepsilon Uniform(0.009, 5) RE standard deviation (fixed at 1 when random effects are disabled)
βc\beta_c Uniform(10−1810^{-18}, 9.99) Community infection rate
βh\beta_h Uniform(10−1810^{-18}, 9.99) Household transmission rate
γ\gamma Uniform(0, 1) Household size parameter (currently fixed at 0)
αinf,k\alpha_{\text{inf},k} Normal(0, 3) Each infectivity covariate effect
αsus,k\alpha_{\text{sus},k} Normal(0, 3) Each susceptibility covariate effect
Weibull shape Uniform(0.1, 10) SI shape (only when estimate_SI = TRUE)
Weibull scale Uniform(0.1, 20) SI scale (only when estimate_SI = TRUE)

The uniform priors on βc\beta_c and βh\beta_h are deliberately wide and effectively non-informative for realistic transmission rates. The Normal(0, 3) prior on covariate effects is weakly informative — it assigns most probability mass to relative risks between exp(−6)≈0.002\exp(-6) \approx 0.002 and exp(6)≈400\exp(6) \approx 400, which is very permissive for epidemiological covariate effects.

MCMC algorithm

Metropolis-Hastings sampler

Parameters are updated one at a time via Metropolis-Hastings with Gaussian random-walk proposals. At iteration bb:

  1. For each parameter θk\theta_k with a free indicator (move[k] = 1):
    1. Propose θk*=θk(b−1)+ϵ\theta_k^* = \theta_k^{(b-1)} + \epsilon, where ϵ∼Normal(0,σk2)\epsilon \sim \text{Normal}(0, \sigma_k^2).
    2. Compute the log acceptance ratio: r=[ℓ(𝛉*)+logπ(𝛉*)]−[ℓ(𝛉(b−1))+logπ(𝛉(b−1))] r = \left[\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}^*) + \log \pi(\boldsymbol{\theta}^*)\right] - \left[\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(b-1)}) + \log \pi(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(b-1)})\right]
    3. Accept with probability min(1,exp(r))\min(1, \exp(r)).

If the proposed parameter falls outside the prior support, the prior log-density is −∞-\infty and the proposal is automatically rejected.

Adaptive proposal tuning

After the first 500 iterations, proposal standard deviations σk\sigma_k are set to the empirical posterior standard deviation of the chain so far, with multiplicative adjustments based on acceptance rates:

Acceptance rate Adjustment
< 10% σk×0.5\sigma_k \times 0.5
10–15% σk×0.8\sigma_k \times 0.8
15–20% σk×0.95\sigma_k \times 0.95
20–30% No change (target range)
30–40% σk×1.05\sigma_k \times 1.05
40–90% σk×1.2\sigma_k \times 1.2
> 90% σk×2.0\sigma_k \times 2.0

This adaptive scheme typically achieves stable acceptance rates within 1000–2000 iterations.

Data augmentation

At each MCMC iteration, after updating all parameters, the following latent variables are updated for each household member via a joint Metropolis-Hastings step:

  1. Infection times: For infected non-index contacts, a new onset time is proposed uniformly over the follow-up window. The proposal is accepted or rejected based on the full household likelihood ratio.

  2. Random effects (when enabled): For each infected individual, a new random effect εj*\varepsilon_j^* is proposed from εj+Normal(0,σε)\varepsilon_j + \text{Normal}(0, \sigma_\varepsilon).

  3. Missing covariates: For individuals with missing factor covariates, a new level is proposed uniformly from all levels of the factor. This is a symmetric proposal, so no correction term is needed.

All three augmentation steps are proposed jointly for each household member and accepted or rejected together. This joint update is more efficient than separate updates because the onset time, random effect, and covariate value are correlated in the posterior. The augmentation step is parallelized across households using RcppParallel.

Serial interval estimation

When estimate_SI = TRUE, two additional parameters (Weibull shape and scale) are added to the MCMC. At each iteration, the serial interval PMF is recomputed from the current Weibull parameters:

w(d)=FW(d+1;k,λ)−FW(d;k,λ),d=1,2,…,14 w(d) = F_W(d + 1; k, \lambda) - F_W(d; k, \lambda), \quad d = 1, 2, \ldots, 14

where FW(⋅;k,λ)F_W(\cdot; k, \lambda) is the Weibull CDF with shape kk and scale λ\lambda. The Weibull parameters are updated via the same Metropolis-Hastings random-walk scheme as the other parameters.

Convergence diagnostics

Because the sampler uses a single chain with adaptive tuning, standard multi-chain diagnostics (such as R̂\hat{R}) are not directly applicable. Instead, we recommend:

  1. Trace plots: plot_diagnostics(fit) shows trace plots and posterior densities for all parameters. Look for stationarity after burn-in and good mixing (no long excursions or sticky regions).

  2. Effective sample size (ESS): plot_diagnostics(fit, show_ess = TRUE) or table_parameters(fit, show_ess = TRUE) report ESS using the initial positive sequence estimator (Geyer, 1992). ESS values below 100 suggest poor mixing; consider increasing n_iteration or investigating identifiability.

  3. Acceptance rates: table_parameters(fit) shows acceptance rates. Rates outside 15–40% indicate poor tuning, which may occur if the burn-in was insufficient for the adaptive scheme to converge. In practice, the default burn-in of 5000 iterations is usually sufficient.

  4. Log-likelihood trace: fit$log_likelihood stores the full log-likelihood trace (before thinning) for visual inspection.

Recommended MCMC settings

For most datasets:

  • n_iteration = 50000: sufficient for convergence with moderate-dimensional models.
  • burnin = 10000: allows the adaptive tuning to stabilize.
  • thinning = 10: reduces autocorrelation in stored samples.

For datasets with many covariates, missing data, or estimate_SI = TRUE, consider longer chains (100,000+ iterations) and examine ESS for each parameter.

Interpretation of parameters

Base parameters

  • community: Reported as a daily probability on the probability scale via 1−exp(−βc)1 - \exp(-\beta_c). This is the daily probability of infection from community sources, independent of household transmission.
  • household: Reported as a daily probability via 1−exp(−βh)1 - \exp(-\beta_h). This is the baseline daily probability of person-to-person transmission within a household from a single infected member at peak serial interval.

Covariate effects

Covariate effects are estimated on the log scale. The summary() method reports both the raw (log-scale) estimates and the exponentiated estimates:

  • exp(αk)>1\exp(\alpha_k) > 1: This level has higher infectivity/susceptibility than the reference.
  • exp(αk)<1\exp(\alpha_k) < 1: This level has lower infectivity/susceptibility than the reference.
  • exp(αk)=1\exp(\alpha_k) = 1: No difference from the reference level.

For example, if the susceptibility effect for age group “6-17” is α̂=−0.3\hat{\alpha} = -0.3 with 95% CrI (−0.8,0.2)(-0.8, 0.2), then exp(α̂)=0.74\exp(\hat{\alpha}) = 0.74 with 95% CrI (0.45,1.22)(0.45, 1.22). This means children aged 6-17 have an estimated 26% lower susceptibility than the reference group, but the credible interval includes 1 (no difference).

Serial interval parameters

When estimate_SI = TRUE, the output includes si_shape and si_scale — the Weibull shape (kk) and scale (λ\lambda) parameters. The mean serial interval is λ⋅Γ(1+1/k)\lambda \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/k) and the variance is λ2[Γ(1+2/k)−Γ(1+1/k)2]\lambda^2[\Gamma(1 + 2/k) - \Gamma(1 + 1/k)^2].

Computational details

  • The likelihood and data augmentation are parallelized across households using RcppParallel (Intel TBB backend), providing significant speedups for large datasets.
  • Forward simulation (simulate_data()) is forced to single-threaded mode because the R random number generator is not thread-safe.
  • The C++ backend uses RcppArmadillo for linear algebra operations.

References

Tsang TK, Lau EHY, Cauchemez S, Cowling BJ. Association between antibody titers and protection against influenza virus infection within households. Journal of Infectious Diseases. 2014;210(5):684–692. https://doi.org/10.1093/infdis/jiu186

Geyer CJ. Practical Markov chain Monte Carlo. Statistical Science. 1992;7(4):473–483.
